channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump and Xi Enter China Summit Distracted by Iran War
item.description: The war in Iran has cast a shadow of uncertainty on both superpowers, dimming early hopes that they could begin to address the larger issues that have frayed their relationship.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump meeting with President Xi Jinping of China in Busan, South Korea, last year. The two leaders are set to meet in Beijing on Wednesday.

item.title: Hegseth to Accompany Trump on Trip to China
item.description: Defense secretaries have previously traveled with presidents on overseas trips, to provide advice and represent the U.S. military.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth arriving to testify before the Senate Appropriations subcommittee on defense.

item.title: Reflecting Pool Repairs Appear Uneven and Behind Schedule, Officials Say
item.description: Work on the iconic National Mall basin appears behind schedule. President Trump initially said he had picked the contractor, then said he did not know it.
item.dc:creator: David A. Fahrenthold and Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: About 35 percent of the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool’s surface had been fully coated as of Sunday, according to a government document. The repair work is supposed to be done by May 22.

item.title: Marty Makary, Trump’s F.D.A. Commissioner, Resigns After Weeks of Pressure
item.description: The agency’s top food official will step in to the role after Dr. Makary privately said he opposed the administration decision to approve flavored vapes.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
item.media:description: Dr. Marty Makary at times tried to advance his own principles alongside the administration’s goals, which at times were at odds with the powerful food, tobacco and pharmaceutical industries.

item.title: How Mamdani and Hochul Are Solving New York City’s Budget Crisis
item.description: A generous influx of state resources from Gov. Kathy Hochul and a new tax on luxury second homes will help Mayor Zohran Mamdani balance a $125 billion budget.
item.dc:creator: Sally Goldenberg, Dana Rubinstein and Benjamin Oreskes
item.media:credit: Victor J. Blue for The New York Times
item.media:description: In his executive budget, Mayor Zohran Mamdani dropped his threat to raise property taxes in New York City.

item.title: CPI Shows Inflation Accelerating to 3.8% Annually in April After Weeks of War in Iran
item.description: The Consumer Price Index rose 3.8% in April from a year earlier as higher energy costs replaced tariffs as the driver of higher prices for Americans.
item.dc:creator: Lydia DePillis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: How an ‘Impossible’ Idea Led to a Pancreatic Cancer Breakthrough
item.description: The new strategy also holds promise for lung and colon tumors. Here’s how scientists discovered it.
item.dc:creator: Gina Kolata and Rebecca Robbins
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times

item.title: P.C.O.S. Has Been Renamed P.M.O.S. Doctors Hope It Will Improve Care for Millions.
item.description: The condition has been renamed polyendocrine metabolic ovarian syndrome, or P.M.O.S., to better reflect its wide-ranging health effects.
item.dc:creator: Maggie Astor
item.media:credit: Getty Images

item.title: More Cancer Patients Are Taking Ivermectin. Mel Gibson and Joe Rogan Might Be Why.
item.description: Prescriptions for antiparasitic drugs spiked after the actor claimed it cured his friends of cancer, a new study finds.
item.dc:creator: Nina Agrawal

item.title: Is Anthropic’s Claude Mythos Really a Cybersecurity Risk?
item.description: Anthropic said that Claude Mythos was too dangerous to release to the public. That claim has reopened an old debate over cybersecurity.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz and Kate Conger
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Anthropic shook up the tech world with the limited release of its Claude Mythos A.I. model.

item.title: A.I. and Humans Battle It Out in a Cybersecurity Showdown
item.description: Experts and college students used A.I. agents to try to break into and defend computer networks in a national competition. The agents did all right on their own, too.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz and Mikayla Whitmore
item.category: University of Texas at San Antonio

item.title: Why So Many Guys Are Obsessed With Testosterone
item.description: From the Trump administration to online influencers, the hormone is increasingly seen as the key to achieving a new male ideal.
item.dc:creator: Azeen Ghorayshi

item.title: What to Know About the New Obsession With Testosterone
item.description: From politics to influencers and beyond, the hormone is being used not just for medical reasons but in pursuit of a new masculine ideal.
item.dc:creator: Azeen Ghorayshi

item.title: How to Eat Through Our List of the Best Restaurants in New York City
item.description: Don’t have the time (or money) to try all 100? Here are a dozen bite-size bucket lists to inspire you.
item.dc:creator: Nikita Richardson, Becky Hughes, Luke Fortney, Mahira Rivers, Ryan Sutton, Eric Asimov, Ligaya Mishan and Rozalina Burkova

item.title: Mysterious Meddling in Democratic Primaries Has G.O.P. Fingerprints
item.description: A new super PAC with ties to Republicans is intervening in Democratic primaries, in one instance helping a sex therapist in Texas accused of antisemitism.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Voters waiting in line to cast ballots in the Texas primary in Austin in March 3.

item.title: Alabama Governor Sets New Primary Elections for Four House Seats
item.description: Gov. Kay Ivey scheduled special House primaries for August after the Supreme Court cleared a path for Alabama to use a 2023 congressional map with one majority-Black district.
item.dc:creator: Emily Cochrane
item.media:credit: Julie Bennett/Associated Press
item.media:description: Gov. Kay Ivey of Alabama set a special election on Tuesday for four House races.

item.title: Raffensperger Campaign Event at Georgia Airport Is Disrupted by ‘Active Threat,’ Bomb Squad on Site
item.description: A suspicious object that disrupted a campaign stop for Brad Raffensperger, the Republican secretary of state in Georgia and a candidate for governor, was not a bomb, officials said.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti and Richard Fausset
item.media:credit: Elijah Nouvelage/Getty Images
item.media:description: Brad Raffensperger’s campaign announced shortly after the incident that his event, at a local airport, had faced an “active threat.”

item.title: Inside the Hantavirus Quarantine Unit in Nebraska Where 15 Americans Wait
item.description: The National Quarantine Unit in Nebraska is now the temporary home for many of the Americans who had been passengers on the MV Hondius.
item.dc:creator: Sonia A. Rao
item.media:credit: Dylan Widger/Getty Images
item.media:description: The Davis Global Center, at the University of Nebraska Medical Center campus in Omaha, houses the National Quarantine Unit.

item.title: In Cleveland, Smokers Are Helping to Keep the Arts Alive
item.description: A novel cigarette tax has generated $270 million for cultural organizations in the Cleveland area, which makes declining smoking rates “a double-edged sword.”
item.dc:creator: Michaela Towfighi and Ross Mantle
item.category: Rock and Roll Hall of Fame and Museum
item.media:description: Playhouse Square, in downtown Cleveland, has used grants from a cigarette tax to explore creating a designated theater district akin to Times Square or the West End.

item.title: YouTube Plays Matchmaker for Sponsors and Stars
item.description: The company is trying to help streaming influencers, who are increasingly being wooed by competitors like Netflix and TikTok.
item.dc:creator: John Koblin
item.media:credit: Brian Karlsson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kareem Rahma, the host of the popular Instagram and TikTok series “Subway Takes.”

item.title: Why Your Life Is Now on Subscriptions
item.description: As companies look to build cash flow and loyalty, everything from heated car seats to earthworm deliveries can become a recurring charge on your credit card.
item.dc:creator: Sopan Deb
item.media:credit: Nam Y. Huh/Associated Press
item.media:description: From streaming services to car features, more companies are moving toward a model that turns customers into lifelong subscribers.

item.title: Why Young People Are Breaking Into Scientology Churches for TikTok
item.description: The police in Los Angeles, New York, Sydney and elsewhere are responding to incidents related to a TikTok trend involving running through Scientology buildings.
item.dc:creator: Madison Malone Kircher
item.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times
item.media:description: A group of teenagers broke a locked door at a Church of Scientology in Midtown Manhattan, the New York Police Department said. It’s part of what has become a global trend.

item.title: Hacky Sack Mounts a Comeback With Gen Z
item.description: Teenagers are booting the game out of the 1990s. “It’s kind of bringing everybody together,” one said.
item.dc:creator: Callie Holtermann

item.title: Oregon Prepares for a Challenging Summer of Water Shortages and High Fire Risk
item.description: After a warm winter that brought more rain than snow, the state’s snowpack was the lowest it has ever been.
item.dc:creator: Amy Graff, Anna Griffin and Loren Elliott
item.media:credit: Loren Elliott for The New York Times

item.title: Wildfires Strike Florida, Georgia and America’s ‘Wood Basket’
item.description: Large fires, fueled by a record breaking drought, have been destroying homes and timber plantations in Southeastern states.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey
item.media:credit: Joe Raedle/Getty Images
item.media:description: A firefighter from Pembroke Pines, Fla., on Monday.

item.title: China Is Much Weaker Than It Seems. That’s the Problem.
item.description: History shows there is no more productive national asset than freedom.
item.dc:creator: Bret Stephens
item.media:credit: Photo Illustration by Evan Hume for The New York Times

item.title: I Have Some Questions for the Democrats Who Want to Run California
item.description: The Opinion columnist Ezra Klein moderates a forum with five of the top Democratic candidates for governor of California.
item.dc:creator: Ezra Klein

item.title: Person Fatally Struck by Frontier Airlines Jet at Denver Airport Is Identified
item.description: The individual, a 41-year-old man, breached a fence at the airport on Friday minutes before being struck by a Frontier Airlines jet, officials said.
item.dc:creator: Christina Morales
item.media:credit: David Zalubowski/Associated Press
item.media:description: The Frontier Airlines jet that fatally struck a person was parked at the airline’s technical operations center north of Denver International Airport on Saturday.

item.title: Justice Department Charges Companies in Baltimore Bridge Collapse
item.description: The criminal charges are the latest legal fallout after the 2024 crash, which killed six men who were working on the Key Bridge.
item.dc:creator: Campbell Robertson
item.media:credit: Erin Schaff/The New York Times
item.media:description: A cargo ship hit the Francis Scott Key Bridge in Baltimore in 2024, leading to the deaths of six people.

item.title: Student Is Fatally Stabbed in University of Washington Housing Complex
item.description: Police officers responding to a call found the student dead in the laundry room of an off-campus residential building. Investigators are now searching for a suspect.
item.dc:creator: Chris Hippensteel
item.media:credit: Seattle Police Department
item.media:description: Police officers are hunting for the person responsible for the killing in a building less than a mile from campus.
